# Knowledge Decay

## The Physics

All recorded knowledge has a half-life. The moment information is captured,
it begins diverging from reality. Market conditions change, personnel rotate,
technology evolves, decisions get superseded. Knowledge that was accurate
yesterday may be misleading today.

Stale information is MORE dangerous than missing information. Missing information
triggers a search. Stale information triggers confident action in the wrong
direction. The consumer trusts it because it exists, never suspecting it has
decayed past usefulness.

## Why It Matters

- **False confidence**: Stale docs produce confident wrong decisions.
- **Compounding error**: Downstream work built on stale knowledge inherits its staleness.
- **Search pollution**: Stale results crowd out current results in any retrieval system.
- **Governance rot**: Unenforced rules teach people that governance is optional.

## Academic/Industry Backing

- Information half-life studies (Arbesman, "The Half-Life of Facts").
- Memory decay curves (Ebbinghaus): biological analog for organizational knowledge loss.
- Technical debt literature: documentation debt compounds exactly like code debt.

## Connected Objects

- JOS-P05 (Source of Truth) — one current source prevents stale duplicates
- JOS-S05 (Governance Object Lifecycle) — lifecycle management prevents decay
- JOS-R04 (Review Currency) — the testable enforcement against decay
